title: Why Earthquakes Happen on Fault Lines

lesson.learn:
Earthquakes happen when stress builds up in Earth's crust until rocks suddenly slip along a fault. The fault line is the surface expression of a break in the crust, but the real action happens where rocks lock, bend, and then release energy. That release travels as seismic waves, which we feel as shaking.

Fault lines matter because they are where plate motion becomes visible. The Earth is always moving; earthquakes are what it looks like when that motion is not smooth.
lesson.simpler: Earthquakes happen when rocks suddenly slip along faults after stress builds up underground.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Fault lines are places where stored-up tectonic stress can suddenly escape.

thirdImageCredit.creator: Ralph L. Dawes, Ph.D. and Cheryl D. Dawes

lesson.deeperDive:
Not every fault behaves the same way. Normal faults, reverse faults, and strike-slip faults each move differently, depending on whether the crust is stretching, compressing, or sliding sideways. Some faults creep slowly, while others lock for years and then rupture in a much larger quake. That variability is one reason earthquake prediction is hard.

Still, the pattern is consistent: stress accumulates, rocks resist, and then the system fails. The shaking is not random. It is the sound of Earth's crust adjusting itself.
lesson.funFact: An earthquake is basically a sudden release of stored rock stress.

people: Engineers - experts in building for shaking
Emergency planners - people who prepare communities
places: Fault zones - where the crust breaks and moves
Subduction zones - places of powerful quakes
Fault scarps - surface traces of movement
years: 1906 - the San Francisco quake raises awareness
Present day - fault monitoring is global
concepts: Fault - a break in rock where movement occurs
Seismic wave - energy released by an earthquake
Slip - sudden movement along a fault
Plate boundary - where tectonic plates meet
